steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures on each side of the comma
steps.content: The main clause "Congress designated a portion of Pennsylvania as the Lackawanna Valley National Heritage Area" is complete. The blank introduces a phrase explaining Congress's reason for that designation.

steps.title: Apply the participial phrase rule
steps.content:
A present participle (-ing form) after a comma correctly adds information modifying the subject of the main clause without creating a new independent clause.

A) they cite — introduces a second full subject ("they"); two independent clauses joined by only a comma = comma splice. Wrong.
B) cited — simple past creates a second independent clause ("Congress cited"), again a comma splice without a coordinating conjunction. Wrong.
C) citing — present participle phrase; modifies the main clause and explains the reason for the designation. No extra subject, no punctuation problem. Correct.
D) has cited — present-perfect verb with an implied subject creates a third independent clause; comma splice. Wrong.

C is correct.
